coredata.dc:title: Boosting the adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity of activated carbon by graphene quantum dots and titanium dioxide
coredata.dc:creator: Tohamy, Hebat-Allah S.
Fathy, Nady A.
El-Sakhawy, Mohamed
Kamel, Samir
coredata.dc:description: This paper is devoted to enhancing the adsorption and photocatalytic efficiency of activated carbon (AC) by formulating new composites with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared from bagasse, followed by embedding titanium dioxide (TiO2) on the surface of GQDs/AC to obtain GQDs-TiO2/AC. Methylene blue (MB) dye was selected as a model dye pollutant which widely used in leather and textile industries. Studies on removing of MB dye using prepared samples included adsorption and photocatalytic runs. Langmuir model fits the adsorption of MB dye by AC, GQDs/AC, and GQDs-TiO2/AC adsorbents with maximum capacities of 215, 285, and 313 mg/g, respectively. The presence of GQDs with and without TiO2 boosted considerably the surface-active sites, as confirmed by FTIR analysis. Photocatalytic runs in presence of H2O2 as an oxidizing agent and GQDs-TiO2/AC were examined using 200, 300, and 400 mg/L of MB dye. The photo-degradation rate of MB dye by GQDs-TiO2/AC was fast, and superior removal efficiencies were reached; 97.6, 87.6, and 85.7 % from these initial concentrations at 30 min, respectively. Hence, this study affirmed that the GQDs-TiO2/AC demonstrates great potential in removing MB dye through adsorption and photo-degradation processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Activated carbon
Graphene quantum dots
Titanium dioxide
Methylene blue
Adsorption
Photo-degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic oxidation studies using H2O2/visible light
Effect of contact time and initial dye concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs at two magnifications.
SEM images of the A) GQDs, B) AC, C) GQDs/AC and D) GQDs-TiO2/AC.
XRD profiles of; A) AC, B) GQDs, C) GQDs/AC, and D) GQDs-TiO2/AC.
FTIR spectra of; A) AC, B) GQDs, C) GQDs/AC, and D) GQDs-TiO2/AC.
Adsorption isotherms of MB dye adsorbed by AC, GQG, and GQDs-TiO2/AC.
(A) Effect of contact time and initial dye concentrations on the removal of MB dye and (B) Pseudo-second order plots at different initial dye concentrations.
Intraparticle diffusion plots obtained from kinetic adsorption of MB dye onto GQDs-TiO2/AC.
Removal of MB dye of Co: 200 mg/L (A), 300 mg/L (B), and 400 mg/L by photo-degradation over the GQDs-TiO2/AC under visible light at constants catalyst dose = 1 g/L, pH = 6, and T = 25 °C.
Langmuir and Freundlich parameters for MB dye by AC, GQG, and GQDs-TiO2/AC.
Comparison of the adsorption capacity (Q, mg/g) of Cr(VI) and MB dye by various adsorbents.
Adsorption kinetic equation and linear fit parameters for the adsorption of MB dye onto GQDs-TiO2/AC (pH = 6 and T = 25 °C).

head.ce:title: Boosting the adsorption capacity and photocatalytic activity of activated carbon by graphene quantum dots and titanium dioxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Cairo, P.O. 12622, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Cairo, P.O. 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physical Chemistry Department, Research Institute of Advanced Materials Technology and Mineral Resources, National Research Centre, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Cairo, P.O. 12622, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Advanced Materials Technology and Mineral Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physical Chemistry Department, Research Institute of Advanced Materials Technology and Mineral Resources, National Research Centre, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Cairo, P.O. 12622, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper is devoted to enhancing the adsorption and photocatalytic efficiency of activated carbon (AC) by formulating new composites with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared from bagasse, followed by embedding titanium dioxide (TiO2) on the surface of GQDs/AC to obtain GQDs-TiO2/AC. Methylene blue (MB) dye was selected as a model dye pollutant which widely used in leather and textile industries. Studies on removing of MB dye using prepared samples included adsorption and photocatalytic runs. Langmuir model fits the adsorption of MB dye by AC, GQDs/AC, and GQDs-TiO2/AC adsorbents with maximum capacities of 215, 285, and 313 mg/g, respectively. The presence of GQDs with and without TiO2 boosted considerably the surface-active sites, as confirmed by FTIR analysis. Photocatalytic runs in presence of H2O2 as an oxidizing agent and GQDs-TiO2/AC were examined using 200, 300, and 400 mg/L of MB dye. The photo-degradation rate of MB dye by GQDs-TiO2/AC was fast, and superior removal efficiencies were reached; 97.6, 87.6, and 85.7 % from these initial concentrations at 30 min, respectively. Hence, this study affirmed that the GQDs-TiO2/AC demonstrates great potential in removing MB dye through adsorption and photo-degradation processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The most reliable source for manufacturing valuable products that require fewer chemical reagents (i.e., eco-friendly) to remove metal ions from wastewater is agriculture waste. Agriculture trash accumulates harms the environment; as a result, recycling is required. A greater attempt has recently been made to utilize agricultural wastes. One of them was the creation of carbon nanostructures using sugarcane bagasse (SCB) as a natural source [1–3]. Carbon nano-materials with a large surface area that have carbon particles with 10 nm in size are known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs). While GQDs are made up of grapheme sheets (G) and CQDs. GQDs have functional groups that include oxygen, such as OH, CO, and COC [1,4,5]. GQDs have been produced by using a variety of techniques, including heating organic molecules hydrothermally and solvothermal, as well as laser ablation of graphite and pyrolytic carbonization. Several studies have shown that using microwave heating is an appropriate strategy for creating quicker and more affordable processes for CQD synthesis [6]. Due to their sustainability, agricultural wastes and carbohydrates are frequently used to produce carbon compounds [7]. GQDs that are known as fluorescent or luminescent carbon nanomaterials can be applied as highly efficient substrates because they exhibit some advantages; e.g., excellent photostability, low cytotoxicity, improved biocompatibility, and confined emission of energy in the fields of sensors, light (energy-from high to low frequency) conversion, cell or bioimaging, photocatalysis, and photovoltaics [8–10]. Recently, GQDs have been used successfully in wastewater treatment due to their biocompatibility, efficiency, and eco-friendliness [11].

Dyes are widely utilized in various sectors such as textile, paper, rubber, plastics, cosmetics, printing, leather tanning, food processing, and dye manufacture [12,13]. For instance, the yearly use of dyes in the textile sector globally exceeds 10,000 tons, with roughly 100 tons being discharged into waterways each year and over 7 × 105 tons of >100,000 industrial dyes are manufactured yearly [14,15]. The release of massive volumes of dyes into water sources has brought problems to worldwide environmental and health concerns, particularly in poor countries. Because of its high biological stability, MB dye poses significant harm to human health if not properly treated [14]. Long-term exposure to MB in the environment causes disorders of the eyes, skin, respiratory system, and gastrointestinal tract, which may raise the risk of cancer in humans. A high concentration of MB also harms the absorption of oxygen from the air into the water, which can impede the respiration process and the growth of aquatic species. Currently, various wastewater technologies such as coagulation, membrane separation, filtration, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs, e.g.; catalytic wet oxidation using H2O2, Fenton oxidation, and photocatalytic oxidation) and adsorption are applied extensively in the removal of dyes [10,12–14,16,17]. However, AOPs can treat large volumes and high concentrations of dyes when included with appropriate catalysts. TiO2 is the most active photocatalyst under the photon energy of 300 nm < λ < 390 nm at the UV range and has a wide band gap whose photogenerated electrons and holes are easy to combine again [18].To enhance the adsorptive affinity and photoactivity of TiO2and to exceed the UV range to the visible range, many efforts have been made by introducing some metal oxides, noble metals, and carbon nanomaterials into the TiO2 matrix or fixing TiO2 particles on an appropriate substrate such as carbon materials to increase the photocatalytic efficiency [19–25]. Recent studies have been carried out with coating TiO2 by CQDs as a novel strategy to reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs, generate reactive oxygen species under light radiation, enhance energy absorption on TiO2, promote electronic transition, and produce more electron-hole pairs, and thus efficient photocatalysts were obtained [26–28]. A combination of TiO2 with CQDs and AC prepared from waste rice noodles under hydrothermal conditions was studied for removal of heavy metals by AC adsorption and some dyes from aqueous solution during visible photo-degradation mode using CQDs/TiO2 [26]. AC materials have a large total surface area and well-developed microporous structure. However, their ability to remove huge dye molecules may be limited due to their micropores. Thus, the chemical modification of the AC surface with chemical agents is essential to boost its adsorption capacity. To enhance the adsorption performance of activated carbons toward the removal of heavy metals and dyes, modification of the surface with copper has been investigated [29,30]. To the best of our knowledge, the attempt to improve and gain further adsorption and photocatalytic properties of AC materials by GQDs and TiO2 has not yet been reported.

Accordingly, the main objective of the current study is dedicated to enhancing the adsorption and photocatalytic efficiency of commercial AC by formulating new composites with GQDs prepared from bagasse (GQDs/AC) and then TiO2 embedded into the surface of GQDs/AC to obtain GQDs-TiO2/AC where they prepared by physical mixing and then followed by heating the resulting solid composites at 450 °C for 2 h under N2 gas flow. The resulting samples were characterized by TEM, FE-SEM, XRD, and FTIR techniques. Furthermore, methylene blue (MB), as the targeted organic pollutant, was used to study the adsorption and photocatalytic activity of the prepared adsorbents.

A commercial charcoal and orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt%,) were supplied from Rasayan (Turkey). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, C19H42BrN, Mw = 364.45 g/mol) and titanium isopropoxide (TIP, Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chitosan ((C6H11NO4)n, Mw = 600,000–800,000 g/mol) was obtained from ACROS. Analytical grade MB dye (C16H18ClN3S, MW 319.85 g/mol) supplied from Fulka was chosen as the synthetic model dye.

Sugarcane bagasse (SCB), as the most available domestic lignocellulosic materials, was kindly provided by Quena Company for Paper Industry, Egypt. Other chemicals used were of analytical grades and used without further purification.

An amount of commercial charcoal was impregnated firstly with 50 v/v% of H3PO4 under stirring for 2 h at 80 °C and kept at room temperature overnight. The black solid was filtered and dried at 100 °C overnight. Then, the dried black sample was transferred to a stainless steel reactor (60 cm in length and inner diameter of 4 cm) and admitted to a temperature-controlled electric furnace. This sample was heated slowly to reach a final temperature of 700 °C for 2 h under flowing nitrogen gas. Then, the cooled product was thoroughly washed with hot water to remove the residual acid until the pH of the filtrate became at ∼6 and dried overnight at 100 °C. The final product was denoted as AC, as prescribed in the previous study [31].

The preparation of GQDs was carried out as described in Rodríguez-Padrónith et al. with little modification. A mixture of SCB (1 g), N,N-dimethyl formamide (20 mL), and citric acid (0.1 g) was placed in a domestic Microwave oven at 750 W for 3 min, and the powder was collected for further analysis [32].

The prepared AC in above section was used as starting material for obtaining the composites of GQDs/AC and GQDs-TiO2/AC. For mixing GQDs and AC, 5 w/w % of the obtained GQDs with respect to the total weight of AC (1.0 g) was dispersed well in 0.08 g CTAB dissolved in 50 mL distilled water under stirring. Upon heating GQDs solution at 60 °C, AC sample dispersed in 2.5 w/w% chitosan as a cross-linker (0.05 g chitosan dissolved in 3 v/v% acetic acid) was added slowly under vigorous stirring and left for 2 h. After rinsing with hot distilled water through filtration, the solid composite sample was taken out and dried at 80 °C overnight. The net weight of AC was increased by about 10 % confirming the composition of AC and GQDs.

On the other hand, the composite of GQDs-TiO2/AC was prepared as procedure of GQDs /AC, in which about 5 wt% of Ti isopropoxide was inserted into GQDs/AC mixture during sol-gel and adjusted pH at 9 using NH4OH solution according to the method reported by Azzam et al. [19]. After that, the mixture was heated at 60 °C under stirring for 2 h. The resulting solid composite was washed, filtered and dried at 80 °C overnight. The weight of AC was increased about 20 % after mixing with both GQDs and TiO2. Then, thermal heat treatment was applied on both composites at 450 °C for 2 h under flowing of N2 gas to form TiO2 in the second one (GQDs -TiO2/AC) and to compare with GQDs /AC at the same conditions.

In this study, High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-1230, Japan) was used to determine the morphology of parent GQDs. In contrast, surface morphology was recorded using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA3 TESCAN).Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded by employing a KBr pressed disc technique (2 mg of sample and 98 mg of KBr) using FTIR 6500 spectrometer (JASCO, Japan) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction patterns were investigated using a modern PAN analytical diffractometer, X' pert PRO model. Nickel-filtered cupper radiation (λ = 1.542 Å) was applied. All the diffraction patterns were examined at room temperature and under constant operating conditions (40 KV & 40 mA).

Batch adsorption experiments were carried out to study the adsorption capacity of the AC and their composites. About 50 mL of MB solutions with different concentrations (20 to 500 mg/L) were conducted with 50 mg of adsorbent at initial pH of dye (∼6) under shaking at 200 rpm and 25 °C for 24 h. The influence of factors such as initial dye concentrations (100 and 300 g/L) and contact time varied from 50 to 180 min were studied. Duplicate absorbance readings were recorded for each experiment. All absorbance results were calculated by taking the mean of two absorbance readings and their relative errors were below 5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic oxidation studies using H2O2/visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of each adsorbent was studied toward the degradation of a cationic dye such as MB dye under both dark and H2O2/visible light irradiation. Before the visible irradiation, the adsorbent and MB dye solution mixture were sonicated for 60 min to attain well dispersion of the adsorbent and then stirred in the dark for a further 60 min at 25 °C to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and to ensure that the removal of dye was completed by the photocatalytic process. The photocatalytic experiments were performed by adding 100 mg of adsorbent in 100 mL of MB dye solution with initial concentrations of 200, 300, and 400 mg/L at initial pH of ∼6 of the dye solution. Then 0.01 M of H2O2 solution was added as oxidizing agent under stirring, and the visible light was irradiated using a household fluorescent lamp of 150 W-Philips. This lamp irradiates outside at a distance of 10 cm from the surface of the dye solution. The temperature of the solution was maintained constant throughout the reaction.

Throughout all adsorption and photocatalytic experiments, the residual dye concentration was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Model PC-2401) with a 1.0 cm length-path cell. Absorbance measurements of the properly diluted filtrates were made at the maximum wavelengths of 664 nm for MB dye.

The uptake amount of dye by the adsorbents was calculated by applying the mass-balance equation;
(1)
q e
=
C o
−
C e
m
V
where q
e is the uptake amount of dye by the adsorbent (mg/g), C
o and C
e are the initial and equilibrium dye concentration (mg/L), respectively. m is the mass of adsorbent (mg) and V is the volume of the dye solution in contact with the adsorbent (mL) [1–3].

Two isotherm equations, Langmuir and Freundlich models, are studied in this work. The applicability of the isotherm equations was compared using the correlation coefficients, R
2 . The Langmuir isotherm equation has been widely applied to describe experimental adsorption data based on the assumption that maximum adsorption corresponds to a saturated monolayer of adsorbate molecules on the adsorbate surface with constant energy and there is no transmigration of adsorbate in the plane of adsorbate surface. The linear form of the Langmuir isotherm equation is represented by the following equation:
(2)
C e
q e
=
1
K L
Q
+
1 Q
C e
where C e is the equilibrium concentration of adsorbate (mg/ L), q e is the amount adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g adsorbent). Q (mg/ g) is the maximum adsorption capacity and K
L (L /mg) is the Langmuir constant related to the adsorption energy. When C e/q e is plotted against C e, a straight line is obtained with slope 1/Q and intercepts 1/ K
L
Q [2,3].

The essential features of Langmuir adsorption isotherm can be expressed in terms of a dimensionless constant called separation factor or equilibrium parameter (R L), which is defined by the following relationship:
(3)
R L
=
1
1 +
C o
K L

The R L value indicates the shape of the isotherm to be irreversible (R L = 0), favorable (0 < R L < 1), linear (R L = 1), or unfavorable isotherm (R L > 1). Furthermore, Gibbs free energy, ∆Go (kJ/mol) = −RT lnKL, for the adsorption process was calculated at 25 °C for the dye. Where R is the gas constant (8.314 J/mol K) and T is the absolute temperature (T = 298 K).

The Freundlich isotherm is the earliest known relationship describing the sorption equation. This fairly satisfactory empirical isotherm can be used for non-ideal sorption that involves heterogeneous surface energy systems and is expressed by the following equation:
(4)
ln
q e
= ln
K F
+ 1 n ln
C e
where K F (mg/g (L/mg)1/n) is roughly an indicator of the adsorption capacity and 1/n is the adsorption intensity. In general, as the K F value increases, the adsorption capacity increases. The magnitude of the exponent, 1/n < 1, indicates the favorability of adsorption [1,2].

HR-TEM images depicted both GQDs, and their particle diameters varied between 2.5 and 7.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 . GQDs appear as a semi-spherical structure of aggregated fine carbon particles as shown by the SEM image (Fig. 2A). From SEM results, it can be shown that the neat GQDs have a fluffy sheets morphology which attributes to the presence of graphene in their structure (Fig. 2A) [1–3]. The AC has an amorphous surface with developed porous structure (Fig. 2B). Its pore size was 14.4 nm. GQDs particles are found to be embedded into pores with pore size 30.93 nm and distributed uniformly on the surface of AC (Fig. 2C). In addition, the insertion of TiO2 particles was occurred over the surface of GQDs/AC (Fig. 2D) with the creation of porous structure (pore size ∼ 31 nm) and rough surface due to formation of TiO2 through heating at 450 °C for 2 h under flowing of N2 gas as indicated in the insert figure (Fig. 2D).This developed porous structure can efficiently encourage the adsorption of MB dye. At the same time, TiO2 can enhance the photoactivity property.

The XRD patterns of AC exhibit two dispersive peaks located at ca.2θ = 23.5° and 43.7° (Fig. 3A), corresponding to the characteristic (002) and (100) diffraction peaks of graphite, respectively [25,33,34]. For GQDs, a typical XRD profile of GQDs has a broad (002) peak centered at ca. 20.8°, which is similar to that of GQDs synthesized by other methods [4,9]. The intensity of both diffraction peaks at 23.5o and 43.7o is increased upon loading GQDs to AC, confirming the insertion of GQDs to the surface of AC. Considerable changes in the XRD profile of GQDs/AC are observed upon the incorporation of TiO2 particles with appearing crystal phases at 25, 37.5, 47.7, 54.5, and 62.3o, which correspond to crystalline planes of (101), (112), (200), (105), (204), and (116) indication for forming TiO2 anatase phase with interlayer spacing distance varied between 0.149 and 0.355 nm (JCPDS No. 21-1272), respectively [33,34]. This result outlined that TiO2 is chemically bonded to the surface of the GQDs/AC matrix [25].

Fig. 4 illustrates the FTIR spectra of GQDs, AC, GQDs/AC, and GQDs-TiO2/AC composites. Absorption bands related to GQDs are centered at 3426, 2916–2871, 1722–1625, 1560–1385, 1252–1038, and 890–535 cm−1 [35]. These are assigned to hydroxyl (OH) group stretching vibration, symmetric and asymmetric stretching of CH, bending vibrations of the CO and CC group, stretching of OCO, COC and CO groups in GO, stretching vibration of carboxyl groups (CO) and ether groups (COC), and the last region is attributed to an out-of-plan of CH bending mode in aromatic rings [1–3,19,34–36].Meanwhile, AC showed absorption bands at 3430, 2916, 2350, 1568, and 1123–1098 cm−1 with less intensity. Such bands are ascribed to the stretching vibration mode of OH, CH, CN, CC, and CO in ethers, esters, hydroxyls, and phenol groups, respectively [2,37]. The intensity of those bands is increased when AC is combined with GQDs with slight shifting to low wavenumber. This behavior confirms the chemical reactivity between both AC and GQDs. In the FTIR spectrum of GQDs-TiO2/AC, it is seen that there is a sharp decrease in the intensity of OH vibration mode, nevertheless; this is a return to replacement of OH by TiOH bond on the surface of GQDs/AC. Moreover, the appearance of a strong and broad absorption band at ca. 600 cm−1is ascribed to the bending vibrations of TiOTi and TiOC bonds, as evident by XRD analysis [19,36].

Table 1 compares the adsorption results calculated from two applied models. From the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption capacity (Q, mg/g) of MB dye was found to be accounted for between 215, 285, and 313 mg/g for AC, GQDs/AC, and GQDs-TiO2/AC, respectively. The adsorption capacity of MB by AC increases more about 32.6 and 45.6 % upon a combination of AC with GQDs and GQDs-TiO2, respectively. This result states that the described surface modification of AC with GQDs and GQDs-TiO2 successfully attained the study's objective. The R
2 values of the Langmuir model reached 0.99 for the adsorption of MB dye over the studied adsorbents suggesting that the Langmuir isotherm can fit the adsorption data. On the other hand, R
2 values obtained from the Freundlich isotherm are low, and the adsorption of MB dye cannot match the Freundlich isotherm. Thus Langmuir isotherm can explain the adsorption of dyes [37–47]. As seen in Table 1, the values of R L < 1 confirmed that prepared adsorbents are highly favorable for the adsorption of basic dye from wastewater under the conditions used in this study. The negative values of ∆Go (−22 to – 25 kJ/mol) affirm the feasibility and spontaneous nature of MB dye adsorption onto the surface of adsorbents [1–3].

Equilibrium adsorption data were plotted in Fig. 5 and showed the adsorption type. L-type (Langmuir) adsorption is obtained on all adsorbents. Thus, this result also confirms the applicability of Langmuir isotherm, confirming the homogeneous nature of adsorbents' surface-active sites, which found to be accessible sites for the accommodation of the dye molecules [1,2]. The smaller 1/n value and higher KF value for GQDs-TiO2/AC, as calculated from the Freundlich model, indicate that the adsorption of MB dye over its surface is more favorable as compared to other adsorbents (i.e., AC and GQDs/AC).

The maximum adsorption capacity of MB dye by GQDs- TiO2/AC is compared to that of other common adsorbents such as hydrochar, fruit peels, clay, activated carbons, and GQDs composite, as listed in Table 2 . Considering the surface properties of each adsorbent, it has been found that the best adsorbent prepared in this study has a good adsorption capacity toward MB dye compared to other adsorbents. Therefore, the prepared AC modified with GQDs and TiO2 may be a talented adsorbent for organic dye removal, especially MB dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time and initial dye concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of contact time and initial dye concentration on the adsorbed amount of dye (qt, mg/g) onto GQDs-TiO2/AC are given in Fig. 6 A and B. With an increase in contact time from 5 to 180 min, the adsorbed amount of dye is increased substantially. Increasing the adsorbed amount of dye with the increasing of the initial concentration from 100 to 300 mg/L reveals higher qt (mg/g) of MB dye. Equilibrium time can be achieved during 30 min over the surface of theGQDs-TiO2/AC adsorbent for 100 and 300 mg/L concentrations of MB dye. The uptake rates of MB dye on GQDs-TiO2/AC were very fast at the first 5 min, which gradually decreased with increasing the time till equilibrium. This finding explains that GQDs-TiO2/AC sample has available and large number of active sites, and then the adsorption rate decreases with the gradual occupancy of these sites [44].

In this section, two kinetic models were applied for analyzing the adsorption of MB dye onto GQDs-TiO2/AC. First, Pseudo-second order kinetic model to estimate the mechanism of adsorption (Fig. 6B). Mathematical form of pseudo-second-order kinetic model is given in Table 3 , where k2 is the pseudo-second-order kinetic adsorption rate constant (g/mg min) [46,47]. The slope and intercept of the linear plots t/qt against t yield the values of 1/qe and 1/k2qe 2 (Fig. 6B). Taking into account the corresponding correlation coefficient (R2) derived from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, the adsorption of MB dye onto the prepared adsorbent is well-fitted with this model. Furthermore, the experimental qe (qe,exp) values are comparable to the corresponding calculated values (qe,calc) computed from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, indicating that the adsorption process is governed by chemisorption [1–3].

Second, an intra-particle mass transfer diffusion model is described by the equation in Table 3. Where, k d is the intra-particle diffusion rate constant (mg/g min0.5), which can be calculated from the slope of the linear plots of q
t versus t
0.5 and C is a constant and calculated from intercept of this plot. The intraparticle diffusion plots are shown in Fig. 7 , observing that straight lines do not pass through the origin point. This behavior confirms that there are two stages of adsorption; i.e., surface adsorption and intraparticle diffusion, that can be controlled the surface adsorption of MB onto GQDs-TiO2/AC, which is in agreement with the previous study [46,47]. This finding was attributed to the strong electrostatic attraction of the dye to the adsorbent surface followed by the diffusion of dye into adsorbent pores, then the slow diffusion under low solute concentrations in the final stage [47].

Since the adsorption process often contains secondary pollution and not all dye is removed in a short time, the photocatalytic oxidation process can provide prompt treatment and harmless products at the end of the process. In this sense, the prepared GQDs-TiO2/AC sample was tested as a photocatalyst containing TiO2 particles in the presence of less amount of oxidizing agent (e.g., 0.01 mol/L of H2O2) to generate fastly the hydroxyl radicals (HO●) for mineralizing toxic organic compounds to H2O and CO2 [18,19,25,45] according to the following reaction paths:
(5)
H 2
O 2
+ hv → 2
HO ●
(6)
e −
TiO 2 − CB
+
H 2
O 2
→
HO ●
+
OH −
where (e− TiO2-CB) refers to electrons generated in the conduction band of TiO2. Accordingly, the produced HO● radicals can attack both the adsorbed and the remained dye molecules in the aqueous solution and then decompose them rapidly.

In this regard, three different initial concentrations of MB dye (i.e., 200, 300, and 400 mg/L) were examined photo-catalytically using GQDs-TiO2/AC under visible light from 5 to 180 min; to explore the photocatalytic properties of the prepared adsorbents and efficiency of the process with comparing to its adsorption efficiency. Fig. 8 illustrates the removal efficiency of dye through photocatalytic oxidation at photo-degradation rate (C/Co) and removal %of MB dye for different concentrations. At Co = 200 mg/L, the photo-degradation rate occurs fastly during 5 min (C/Co = 0.217 and% removal = 78.3 %) to be 0.024 and 97.6 % at 30 min and then becomes 0 and 100 % as complete removal at 180 min. Increasing concentration to 300 mg/L, the removal rate of MB dye decreases slightly to be C/Co = 0.124 of 87.6 % at 30 min; however, with increasing time, 99.6 % removal was attained at 180 min. After that, a further increase in the concentration to 400 mg/L leads to another slight decrease compared to 300 mg/L, where about 85.8 and 96 % of MB dye were degraded at 30 and 180 min, respectively. These results affirmed the superior photocatalytic efficiency of the prepared GQDs-TiO2/AC photocatalyst. This result may be attributed to its formulation, which consists of TiO2 particles and GQDs combined along the AC surface as well as is attributed to the high adsorption capability [45]. In recent work, it has been reported that the high photocatalytic activity of biochar-based magnetic photocatalyst toward malachite green and rhodamine B dyes was due to its high adsorption capacity (576.7 and 334.9 mg/g, respectively) [45]. Both AC and GQDs/AC have tested their photocatalytic properties toward 200 mg/L of MB dye, but there was no notable degradation along contact time of 4 h (240 min) under visible light (not shown in Figs), approximately; from 2 to 8 % by AC and 5 to 11 % by GQDs/AC within 5–240 min. From this observation, the presence of TiO2 activates the oxidation process of H2O2 to form hydroxyl radicals in the solution, as well as the visible light, increasing the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes on the surface of GQDs-TiO2/AC. The role of GQDs is to enhance the interaction of TiO2 particles along the surface of AC and to retard the combination of electrons–holes again. GQDs in contact with TiO2 captured the photogenerated electrons (e− CB), increased the separation of electrons–holes, and retarded their recombination. Electrons and holes initiated the formation of HO● radicals [19]. Electrons are transferred to the surface of AC, which acts as donors of electrons to oxidize H2O2 into HO● radicals. HO● radicals produced in this process led to a sufficient oxidative potential to degrade MB dye molecules. Generally, the mineralization pathway of MB dye contains the destruction of its structure into aromatic rings and aliphatic acids as intermediates during the photocatalytic degradation of dyes in presence of TiO2 and GQDs. Moreover, the heteroatoms (e.g., N and S) in the dye structure will be converted into nitrate and sulfate anions as well as harmless gaseous CO2 and H2O under photocatalytic processes [19,45].

In this study, we have successfully synthesized activated carbon-based composites loaded with 10 wt% of GQDs and TiO2 to boost up and gain further adsorption and photocatalytic properties to the AC as adsorbents used widely in wastewater purification. The obtained adsorbents, i.e., GQDs/AC and GQDs-TiO2/AC; can treat large concentrations of MB dye (100–500 mg/L) and exhibited higher adsorption capacity of 285 and 313 mg/g, respectively, than that of AC itself (215 mg/g). The adsorption of MB dye over the samples was aligned with the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order kinetic model and possessed a better correlation coefficient with an R 2 > 0.99. GQDs-TiO2/AC possessed synergized effects related to the combination of GQDs and TiO2, which led to a superior photoactivity toward degradation of 200–400 mg/L of MB dye within 30 min; about 97.6 to 85.8 % was degraded under visible irradiation and 0.01 mol of H2O2. Based on these results, conclusively, it can be pointed out that the synthesized here opens the way to prepare efficient adsorbents and catalysts based on AC to use in broad and significant applications. Combining TiO2 and GQDs with AC improves adsorption and photo-degradation processes. Therefore, these materials are considered promising for purifying water from harmful dyes.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the National Research Center, Egypt, for the financial support of this research activity.
